unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you will explore myths and legends — the timeless tales that people have told for hundreds and even thousands of years. Myths are ancient stories that explain why things in nature happen (such as why the sun rises or why the sea is salty). They often involve gods, spirits, or supernatural beings. Legends are stories loosely based on real events or real people from history, but with unlikely or exaggerated details added over time. Both myths and legends have been passed down through oral tradition, meaning people told them aloud long before anyone wrote them down.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners retelling myths and legends for an audience

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He made a hasty decision to leave the game early.
practiceSentences: He made a hasty decision to leave the game early.
The word hasty belongs to our feelings and reactions vocabulary.
I can explain hasty in my own words.
I can connect hasty to this unit's texts.
I can use hasty in a clear Grade 5 sentence.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - a - s - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hasty', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Telling Timeless Tales.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain the difference between a myth and a legend.
Identify the key features of classic tales: oral tradition, timeless settings, moral or explanatory purpose, and larger-than-life characters.
Use defining relative clauses to add essential information to your sentences.
Use the present continuous tense to talk about future plans and arrangements.
Compare things using comparative adjectives (e.g., more reliable, hotter, wiser).
Identify and use opposites (antonyms) such as dreadful/delightful and miserable/prosperous.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Match verbs with nouns correctly in collocations (e.g., make a decision, face an outcome).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write your own short legend with a clear beginning, middle and end.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Telling Timeless Tales: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In this unit you will explore myths and legends — the timeless tales that people have told for hundreds and even thousands of years. Myths are ancient stories that explain why things in nature happen (such as why the sun rises or why the sea is salty). They often involve gods, spirits, or supernatural beings. Legends are stories loosely based on real events or real people from history, but with unlikely or exaggerated details added over time. Both myths and legends have been passed down through oral tradition, meaning people told them aloud long before anyone wrote them down.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain the difference between a myth and a legend.
Identify the key features of classic tales: oral tradition, timeless settings, moral or explanatory purpose, and larger-than-life characters.
Use defining relative clauses to add essential information to your sentences.
Use the present continuous tense to talk about future plans and arrangements.
Compare things using comparative adjectives (e.g., more reliable, hotter, wiser).
Identify and use opposites (antonyms) such as dreadful/delightful and miserable/prosperous.
Match verbs with nouns correctly in collocations (e.g., make a decision, face an outcome).
Write your own short legend with a clear beginning, middle and end.
Read, discuss and retell myths and legends with expression and detail.
Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find all 25 vocabulary words in your separate Vocabulary file, but these are the ones to learn first.
Open your Vocabulary file to study the full list of 25 words with definitions, examples and practice activities.
Reading: The Legend of the Whispering Dunes
Read the following legend carefully. It is set in the deserts of East Africa. As you read, look for features of a legend: a hero, a quest, unlikely events and a lesson at the end.
Samira remembered a legend her grandmother had told her. It spoke of a hidden spring that lay beneath the Whispering Dunes — sand hills that made a low humming sound when the wind blew across them. The spring, the story said, would reveal itself only to someone who was patient and reliable, someone who could follow the sound without making a hasty choice.

readings.title: Telling Timeless Tales: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"I will go," Samira told the elders. Her cousin Ishmael, who was anxious but brave, joined her. Together they set off on a quest across the burning sand.
By the second day, the heat was dreadful. Ishmael spotted a pool of blue water shimmering in the distance. "Let us run to it!" he cried. But Samira shook her head. "That is a mirage — a trick of the light. The spring we seek is the one that whispers. Listen." Ishmael was reluctant to pass up the blue shimmer, but he trusted Samira.
On the third morning, Samira heard a faint hum. The dunes ahead were singing. She walked slowly, placing each foot with care, following the sound. At last she reached a low hollow between two tall dunes. The sand there was dark and damp. Samira knelt and dug with her hands. Cool, clear water bubbled up — the hidden spring!
Isaac was grateful beyond words. They filled every goatskin bag and hurried back to the village. The people were delightful in their celebrations, and the elders called Samira's deed honorable.
From that day, the village marked the path to the Whispering Dunes with stones. And the elders added a new lesson to the old legend: "A reliable heart that listens carefully will always find what a hasty foot runs past."
The legend of the Whispering Dunes is still told today, around fires on cool desert evenings, reminding every generation that patience and trust are worth more than speed.
Answer these questions in full sentences. Look back at the passage to find the evidence.
Is this story a myth or a legend? Give two reasons for your answer.
What character qualities does Samira show? List at least three adjectives and support each with evidence from the text.
Myths and legends are both types of classic tales, but they are not the same. Let us look at the differences and the things they share.

readings.title: The Hasty Route, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Adventure fiction with character growth
Setting: A desert expedition in Mauritania
Moral: Thoughtful leadership and dependable teamwork matter more than speed in reaching a goal
In the hot, golden deserts of Mauritania, a team of young explorers prepared for a journey across the Adrar Plateau. They were led by twelve-year-old Hannah, a careful thinker, and her cousin Isaac, who was brave — but often made hasty decisions.
“This is a map of the route we’re taking,” Hannah said as she showed the group their plan. “We’re documenting sand-dwelling animals and ancient wells. We’re also testing our new water-purifying backpack.”
Isaac waved a hand. “We should skip the first stop. It’s slower. Let’s head straight to the mountain site.”
Hannah looked at him. “That’s a reasonable idea if the weather holds. But the guide said sandstorms are common today.”
“I’m not reluctant to take risks,” Isaac said forcefully. “Let’s move quickly before it gets hotter!”
The group hesitated. Hannah studied the clouds. “We’re documenting the full route tomorrow. Today’s walk is only a check.”
By noon, the sun was high, and the wind picked up. The group had reached a small ridge where the air grew cooler and surprisingly pleasant. They ate lunch under a large rock, sharing stories and laughing.
“I’m grateful we packed shade cloths,” said Musa, the team’s youngest.
“I’m grateful I listened to Hannah,” added Ibrahim. “This stop is cooler than the other side.”
Suddenly, a loud sound rolled across the dunes — wind, fast and heavy.
They scrambled to cover their supplies and crouch under the cloths. The sand hit them like needles.
“It’s dreadful,” coughed Musa. “We should’ve stayed back.”
“No,” said Hannah. “We just need to wait it out.”

readings.title: The Hasty Route, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
After twenty minutes, the wind died down. The world around them looked different. Their footprints were gone, and so was the second map — blown away.
“We’re off-track now,” said Isaac, brushing sand off his pack. “Maybe this is my fault.”
Hannah didn’t blame him. “It was a justifiable mistake. You wanted to save time. But now we need to be reliable and fix it.”
They checked their compass and followed shadows to rejoin the path.
Suddenly, they noticed a round stone structure ahead.
Hannah smiled. “It’s an ancient well. We found it!”
The group approached slowly. Around the well were carvings of animals, maps, and a mysterious pattern that glowed slightly in the sun.
Hannah knelt down. “These are symbols used by early Berber traders. This is a remarkable find!”
“Do you think they camped here?” Isaac asked.
“Possibly,” Hannah said. “There’s evidence of firestones and crushed herbs. Maybe it was once a stop on a trading route.”
Ishmael leaned forward. “We should collect a soil sample. That could help us prove how old this well is.”
Adam scribbled notes. “This is becoming more than a school project — it feels like a real expedition.”
Hannah grinned. “It is. And we’re documenting history.”
The group took photos, sketched the site, and filtered some well water.
“Let’s label this site: ‘Desert Point 4,’” said Hannah. “We’re documenting this tomorrow, right after we revisit the southern path.”
“Yes,” Hannah said. “We’re presenting the full trail report to the science club next week.”
“I’m still feeling a bit anxious about our return walk,” said Isaac.
“I’ll lead that part,” said Ibrahim. “It’ll be more pleasant now that we know the path.”
Back at camp that night, they compared the two paths. Hannah wrote:

readings.title: The Hasty Route, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“Route A was more comfortable, less dangerous, and full of ancient clues. Route B was shorter but hotter and riskier. The dreadful wind made it miserable.”
“I was too forceful in my choice. Hannah’s slower plan was wiser. Her idea to explore carefully led to a profitable find.”
Later, around the fire, they sipped warm tea. Musa asked, “Do you think we’ll get chosen to present at the regional fair?”
“I think we’ve got a good chance,” Hannah replied. “Our site is unique, and we worked well together.”
Isaac nodded. “We disagreed, but we kept moving. That’s what made it honorable.”
The next day, the team began preparing their presentation.
“We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4,” said Hannah.
“And the decisions that taught them patience,” Isaac added.
They printed photos, diagrams, and their annotated map. The report included a defining clause:
“The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before.”
At the school fair, judges asked, “Was the storm a mistake?”
“No,” Hannah replied. “It was part of the journey.”
“And would you do it again?” they asked.
“Only with this team,” she smiled. “They’re the most faithful, most honorable crew imaginable.”
The story uses all 25 vocabulary words from Unit 4. Here are the ones to pay special attention to:
Find these examples in the story:
"The storm that delayed us led to a well which hadn’t been recorded before."
"...the explorers who found Desert Point 4..."
"...the decisions that taught them patience..."
The characters use the present continuous to talk about things already arranged:
"We’re documenting the full route tomorrow."
"We’re presenting the full trail report next week."
"We’re writing about the explorers who found Desert Point 4."

comprehension.question: Long ago, on the edge of the great Ogaden desert, there lived a girl named Samira who was known for her remarkable memory. The elders said she could remember every word of every story she had ever heard. One dry season, the wells that supplied the village began to fail, one after another. The phenomenon puzzled everyone. Where had the water gone?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an accurate complete-sentence response supported by a relevant detail from the selected text.
comprehension.explanation: The learner should identify relevant text evidence and explain the answer clearly.

comprehension.explanation: The learner should identify relevant text evidence and explain the answer clearly.

grammar.title: What Are Defining Relative Clauses?
grammar.explanation:
A defining relative clause is a group of words that gives us essential information about a noun. It tells us exactly which person, place or thing we are talking about. Without the clause, the sentence would be unclear or incomplete.
Look at this example:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A defining relative clause is a group of words that gives us essential information about a noun. It tells us exactly which person, place or thing we are talking about. Without the clause, the sentence would be unclear or incomplete.
Look at this example:
"The explorer who made the remarkable discovery shared it with his team."
The words "who made the remarkable discovery" form a defining relative clause. They tell us which explorer we mean. If we remove them, the sentence becomes: "The explorer shared it with his team" — and we do not know which explorer.
Why Does This Matter?
Defining relative clauses make your writing clearer and more detailed. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can combine them into one smooth sentence that gives the reader all the information at once. This is especially important in storytelling, report writing and explanations.
The Relative Pronouns: Who, Which, That
A defining relative clause always starts with a relative pronoun. There are three main ones to learn:
Who
Use who when talking about people.
The girl who found the spring saved the village.
The teacher who loves science teaches us every day.
The student who finished the test early helped others.
Which
Use which when talking about things (not people).
The book which was on the table is mine.
The spring which lay beneath the dunes was hidden for centuries.
The movie which won the award was fantastic.
That
Use that for people or things. It is the most flexible relative pronoun and can often replace who or which.
The cat that chased the mouse ran up the tree.
The path that Samira followed was safe.
The storm that delayed us led to an amazing discovery.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are defining relative clauses? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Build a Defining Relative Clause
grammar.explanation:
Follow these four steps:
Identify the noun you want to describe. Example: "the explorer".
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Follow these four steps:
Identify the noun you want to describe. Example: "the explorer".
Choose the right relative pronoun: who for people, which for things, that for either.
Add a clause that gives essential information about that noun. Example: "who made the remarkable discovery".
Place the clause right after the noun. Example: "The explorer who made the remarkable discovery shared it with his team."
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to build a defining relative clause in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Practice: Combine Two Sentences
grammar.explanation:
You can use a defining relative clause to join two short sentences into one:
Short sentences: The dog barked all night. It belongs to my neighbour.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
You can use a defining relative clause to join two short sentences into one:
Short sentences: The dog barked all night. It belongs to my neighbour.
Combined: The dog that barked all night belongs to my neighbour.
Short sentences: The teacher told us the story. She is very kind.
Combined: The teacher who told us the story is very kind.
The No-Comma Rule
This is the most important rule to remember:
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply practice: combine two sentences in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Defining relative clauses do NOT use commas.
grammar.explanation:
The information inside a defining relative clause is essential — it tells us which person or thing we mean. Because it is essential, we do not separate it from the rest of the sentence with commas.
Correct vs Incorrect
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The information inside a defining relative clause is essential — it tells us which person or thing we mean. Because it is essential, we do not separate it from the rest of the sentence with commas.
Correct vs Incorrect
CORRECT: The boy who is wearing a red hat is my brother.
INCORRECT: The boy, who is wearing a red hat, is my brother.
(The incorrect version, with commas, is actually a different type of clause called a non-defining relative clause. You will learn about that later. For now, remember: no commas in defining relative clauses.)
Memory Trick: The 3 W’s
Remember these three words:
Who → for people
Which → for things
That → for either (people or things)
Think of it like picking the right key for the right door. If the door is labelled "people", use who. If it is labelled "things", use which. If you are not sure, that is like a master key — it works on both doors!
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply defining relative clauses do not use commas. in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Adding Commas
Wrong: The boy, who is wearing a red hat, is my brother.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Adding Commas
Wrong: The boy, who is wearing a red hat, is my brother.
Right: The boy who is wearing a red hat is my brother.
Fix: Remember that defining relative clauses give essential information. No commas needed.
Mistake 2: Using the Wrong Pronoun
Wrong: The book who I read was interesting.
Right: The book which I read was interesting. (Or: The book that I read...)
Fix: Ask yourself: Am I talking about a person or a thing? Use who for people, which for things.
Mistake 3: Putting the Clause in the Wrong Place
Wrong: Who made the discovery the explorer shared the map.
Right: The explorer who made the discovery shared the map.
Fix: The relative clause must come immediately after the noun it describes.
Exercises
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blank
Choose the correct relative pronoun (who, which or that) for each sentence.
The dog ______ barked all night kept everyone awake.
The singer ______ won the contest is very talented.
The game ______ we played yesterday was exciting.
The woman ______ lives next door is a doctor.
The cake ______ my mother baked was delicious.
The children ______ arrived early got the best seats.
The story ______ the teacher told was about a brave explorer.
The car ______ is parked outside belongs to my uncle.
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
Join each pair of sentences using a defining relative clause.
The boy won the race. He trains every morning.
The house is very old. It stands on the hill.
The teacher is strict but fair. She teaches us maths.
The river flows through our town. It is very wide.
The man helped us find the way. He was wearing a blue jacket.
The legend teaches us about patience. It was told by the elders.
Exercise 3: Spot the Mistake
Each sentence below has a mistake. Find it and write the correct version.
The girl, who scored the goal, celebrated with her team.
The book who I borrowed was very interesting.
Made the discovery the scientist won an award.
The cat which it caught the mouse was very fast.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write five sentences of your own. Each sentence must contain a defining relative clause. Use each of these relative pronouns at least once: who, which, that.
Try to use vocabulary words from Unit 4 in your sentences (e.g., remarkable, mysterious, reliable, hasty, dreadful, grateful).
Exercise 5: Story Sentences
Go back to the story "The Hasty Route" (in your Story file). Find and copy out three sentences that contain defining relative clauses. Underline the clause in each one.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 5 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a two-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark four changes and explain how two changes improve clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Retell a Legend
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the legends you have read in this unit ("The Legend of the Whispering Dunes", "The Hasty Route" from your Story file, or your own legend). Practise retelling it aloud.
When you retell the legend, try to:
Use a classic opening ("Long ago..." or "In ancient times...").
Change your voice for different characters.
Use expression and body language to make the story exciting.
Include at least one defining relative clause when you speak.
End with the moral or lesson of the story.
Record yourself if you can, then listen back. Did you speak clearly? Did you use expression? Ask your AI tutor for feedback.
Activity 2: Myth or Legend?
Your AI tutor will tell you a short classic tale. Your job is to decide: is it a myth or a legend? Explain your answer using the features you have learned.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise retell a legend and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Group Discussion (or Partner Talk)
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Discuss these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor:
Why do you think people create legends about real places and real people?
What makes a story last for hundreds of years?
If you could create a legend about your own town or city, what would it be about?
Do you think myths are just made-up stories, or do they teach us something important?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise group discussion (or partner talk) and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Dramatic Reading
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Pick a scene from "The Hasty Route" (your Story file) that has dialogue between two or more characters. Read it aloud with a partner or record yourself doing both voices. Focus on:
Using different tones for different characters (e.g., Hannah is calm and thoughtful; Isaac is energetic and sometimes hasty).
Pausing at commas and full stops for natural rhythm.
Adding feeling to words like "dreadful", "grateful" and "remarkable".
Extra Practice: Matching Verbs with Nouns
In English, some verbs work best with specific nouns. These are called collocations. Using the right verb-noun pair makes your English sound natural.
Common Collocations from This Unit
Practice: Match the Verb to the Noun
Draw a line or write the correct verb next to each noun.
______ a justifiable decision (make / do / take)
______ a dreadful outcome (face / give / put)
______ a delightful experience (share / throw / bring)
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise dramatic reading and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Write Your Own Legend
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Now it is your turn to become a storyteller! You are going to write your own short legend. A legend is a story that is loosely based on real events or places, features a hero or heroine, includes unlikely or exaggerated events, and teaches a lesson.
Step 1: Plan Your Legend
Before you start writing, answer these planning questions:
Setting: Where and when does your legend take place? (Think of a real place you know — a desert, a coast, a mountain, a city.)
Hero/Heroine: Who is the main character? What makes them special or admirable?
Quest or Problem: What challenge does the hero face? What are they searching for or trying to achieve?
Unlikely Event: What surprising, exaggerated or unlikely thing happens during the story?
Lesson/Moral: What message or wisdom does the legend teach?
Step 2: Organise Your Story
Use this simple structure:
Beginning: Introduce the setting, the hero, and the problem or quest. Start with a classic opening like "Long ago..." or "In the days when..."
Middle: Show the hero facing challenges. Include dialogue, description and at least one unlikely event. Use defining relative clauses to add detail.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write your own legend.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Here is a short example to show you the kind of legend you might write:
The Shepherd Who Tamed the Wind
Long ago, in the highlands near Harar, there lived a shepherd named Noah who was reliable and kind. Every morning he led his flock to the green meadows that lay beyond the rocky ridge.
One season, a dreadful wind began to blow without stopping. The grass dried, the wells shrank, and the animals grew thin. The elders said it was hopeless, but Noah refused to give up.
He set off on a quest to find the cave where the wind was born. After three days, he reached a cave that hummed and roared. Inside, he found a bird with silver feathers. "Why do you blow so hard?" he asked.
"I am lonely," the bird replied. "No one visits me."
Noah promised to visit the bird every full moon. In return, the bird calmed the wind. The grass grew green again, and the village was grateful beyond words.
The elders called Noah honorable, and they added this lesson to their stories: "A kind word can calm the fiercest storm."
Notice how this model uses a classic opening, a hero on a quest, an unlikely event (a talking bird), dialogue, defining relative clauses, vocabulary words, and a moral at the end.
Extra Grammar: Present Continuous for Future Plans
When we talk about something that is already planned or arranged for the future, we often use the present continuous tense (am/is/are + verb-ing) with a future time expression.
Examples

writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'hasty' mean?
quizzes.options: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully. | Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Very angry or mad. | Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully.
quizzes.explanation: He made a hasty decision to leave the game early.

quizzes.question: What does 'reluctant' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily annoyed or made angry. | Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Not wanting to do something or feeling unsure about it. | Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not wanting to do something or feeling unsure about it.
quizzes.explanation: She was reluctant to try the new food because she didn't know if she would like it.

quizzes.question: What does 'furious' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily annoyed or made angry. | Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Feeling very sad or unhappy. | Very angry or mad.
quizzes.explanation: She was furious when her brother broke her favourite toy.

quizzes.question: What does 'anxious' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily annoyed or made angry. | Feeling very sad or unhappy. | Nice and enjoyable. | Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Worried or nervous about something that might happen.
quizzes.explanation: I felt anxious before my big test at school.

quizzes.question: What does 'grateful' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. | Feeling very sad or unhappy. | Nice and enjoyable. | Very bad or unpleasant.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done.
quizzes.explanation: I am grateful for my friends who help me with my homework.

quizzes.question: What does 'irritable' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily annoyed or made angry. | Nice and enjoyable. | Very bad or unpleasant. | Very pleasing and enjoyable.
quizzes.explanation: When I don't get enough sleep, I feel irritable and want to be alone.

quizzes.question: What does 'miserable' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling very sad or unhappy. | Very bad or unpleasant. | Very beautiful and wonderful. | Very pleasing and enjoyable.
quizzes.explanation: After losing his favourite toy, he felt miserable all day.

quizzes.question: What does 'pleasant' mean?
quizzes.options: Nice and enjoyable. | Something that is strange or hard to understand. | Very beautiful and wonderful. | Very pleasing and enjoyable.
quizzes.explanation: The weather today is very pleasant for a picnic.

quizzes.question: What does 'dreadful' mean?
quizzes.options: Important or meaningful. | Something that is strange or hard to understand. | Very bad or unpleasant. | Very beautiful and wonderful.
quizzes.explanation: The weather was so dreadful that we had to stay inside all day.

quizzes.question: What does 'delightful' mean?
quizzes.options: Important or meaningful. | Something that is strange or hard to understand. | Very pleasing and enjoyable. | Very special or unusual; something that stands out.
quizzes.explanation: The cake was so delightful that everyone wanted a second piece.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"What is the difference between a myth and a legend? Can you give me examples?"
"Can you tell me a short myth that explains something in nature?"
"Why did people tell stories aloud before writing was invented?"
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you read this legend with me and help me with the hard words?"
"What makes this story a legend instead of a myth?"
"Can you help me find the defining relative clause hidden in the passage?"
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you check my answers to the comprehension questions?"
"I am not sure if this is a myth or a legend. Can you help me decide?"
"Can you give me another example of a natural phenomenon that a myth might explain?"
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
"Can you give me a myth and a legend side by side so I can compare them?"
"Is the story of the Whispering Dunes a myth or a legend? Help me use the checklist."

"Can you give me five sentences with defining relative clauses for me to identify?"

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Doing something too quickly without thinking carefully. He made a hasty decision to leave the game early.
Not wanting to do something or feeling unsure about it. She was reluctant to try the new food because she didn't know if she would like it.
Very angry or mad. She was furious when her brother broke her favourite toy.
Worried or nervous about something that might happen. I felt anxious before my big test at school.
Feeling happy and thankful for something nice someone has done. I am grateful for my friends who help me with my homework.
Easily annoyed or made angry. When I don't get enough sleep, I feel irritable and want to be alone.
Feeling very sad or unhappy. After losing his favourite toy, he felt miserable all day.
Nice and enjoyable. The weather today is very pleasant for a picnic.
Very bad or unpleasant. The weather was so dreadful that we had to stay inside all day.
Very pleasing and enjoyable. The cake was so delightful that everyone wanted a second piece.
